title: Financial Markets
summary: Financial Markets are the platform where buyers and sellers trade financial instruments — they channel funds from surplus (households) to deficit (businesses).
Two types: Money Market (short-term, < 1 year) and Capital Market (long-term, > 1 year).
Capital Market has two parts: Primary Market (company sells new securities to investors — IPO) and Secondary Market (investors trade existing securities — Stock Exchange).
Money Market instruments: Treasury Bills, Commercial Paper, Call Money, Certificate of Deposit, Commercial Bills.
Stock Exchange: An organised market for buying/selling existing securities. Functions: Liquidity, Price discovery, Safety, Economic barometer.
Trading procedure: Select broker → Open Demat Account → Place order → Match & Execute → Contract note → Settlement (T+1).
SEBI (Securities and Exchange Board of India): Regulates the securities market. Protects investors, regulates intermediaries, develops the market.

teacher_tip.title: AKSpected Shortcut
teacher_tip.text: 'New shares issued by company' → Primary Market. 'Shares traded between investors' → Secondary Market. 'Short-term, less than 1 year' → Money Market. 'Government short-term borrowing' → Treasury Bills. 'Protects investors' → SEBI. Stock exchanges in India: BSE (Bombay Stock Exchange — oldest in Asia) and NSE (National Stock Exchange).

definition.text: A market that facilitates the transfer of funds from those who have surplus (households) to those who need them (businesses), through various financial instruments.

sections.title: Money Market
sections.content: Market for short-term funds (maturity up to 1 year). Deals in near-cash instruments.
points: Treasury Bills (T-Bills): Issued by RBI on behalf of the government. Short-term borrowing. Available in 91, 182, and 364 day maturities. Sold at discount, redeemed at par.
Commercial Paper: Unsecured promissory note by large creditworthy companies. 15 days to 1 year maturity.
Call Money: Short-term loans between banks. Can be recalled at any time (1 to 15 days). Rate is called 'call rate'.
Certificate of Deposit: Unsecured, negotiable instrument issued by banks to individuals and institutions.
Commercial Bills: Bill of exchange used in credit transactions. Can be discounted with a bank for immediate payment.

sections.title: Capital Market — Primary vs Secondary
points: Primary Market (New Issue Market): Company issues new securities directly to investors (IPO/FPO). Funds go to the company. Price fixed by company. No fixed location.

Secondary Market (Stock Exchange): Investors buy/sell existing securities among themselves. Funds go to selling investor, not the company. Price determined by demand and supply. Has a fixed location (physical or electronic).

sections.title: Stock Exchange — Functions & Trading
points: Functions: Provides liquidity and marketability, Price discovery through demand-supply, Safety of transactions (regulated by SEBI), Acts as economic barometer (reflects economic health), Facilitates capital formation.
Trading Procedure:
1. Select a SEBI-registered broker/sub-broker.
2. Open a Demat (Dematerialised) account with a depository.
3. Place buy/sell order (specifying company, quantity, price).
4. Order is matched electronically at best price.
5. Trade is executed and Contract Note is issued.
6. Settlement: Shares transferred to buyer's Demat account; payment settled (T+1 cycle).

sections.title: SEBI — Securities and Exchange Board of India
sections.content: Established in 1988, given statutory powers in 1992. Headquarters: Mumbai.
points: Objectives: (1) Protect the interests of investors. (2) Promote the development of the securities market. (3) Regulate the securities market.
Functions:
• Protective: Prohibits insider trading, prohibits fraudulent practices, promotes fair practices, educates investors.
• Developmental: Promotes training of intermediaries, allows internet trading, allows underwriting optional.
• Regulatory: Registers and regulates brokers, merchant bankers, mutual funds. Conducts inspections and audits.

tables.title: Primary Market vs Secondary Market
rows: New securities issued for first time

questions.answer: RBI on behalf of the Central Government. They are short-term money market instruments.

questions.text: State any three functions of a Stock Exchange.
questions.answer:
(i) Provides liquidity: Investors can easily buy/sell securities at any time.
(ii) Price discovery: Prices are determined by demand and supply forces transparently.
(iii) Economic barometer: Rise/fall in share prices reflects the economic health of the country.

questions.text: Distinguish between Primary Market and Secondary Market.
questions.answer:
(1) Primary deals in new securities; Secondary deals in existing securities.
(2) In primary, funds go to the company; in secondary, funds go to the selling investor.
(3) Primary has no fixed location; Secondary operates through stock exchanges (BSE, NSE).
(4) In primary, price is fixed by the company; in secondary, price fluctuates with demand and supply.

pyqs.text: Explain any three protective functions of SEBI.
pyqs.answer:
(i) Prohibits insider trading — prevents people with confidential information from unfairly profiting.
(ii) Prohibits fraudulent practices — ensures no manipulation of share prices.
(iii) Educates investors — promotes awareness about investment risks and rights.

related_chapters.reason: Capital market is where firms raise long-term funds
